lesson.menu_title: Numbers That Matter -- Ordinals, Dates, and Time in the Seoul Agenda
lesson.menu_desc: Read and present an official agenda: ordinal numbers (first, seventh, fifteenth), dates in American English (November 7th), and the in / on / at system for time (in November, on the seventh, at ten o'clock). The heart of the class is the official agenda of the Seoul visit in November -- a reading with gist and true/false -- plus a voicemail from Bruno confirming the dates and Maria Clara presenting her own schedule. Vocabulary: session, keynote, opening, ceremony, venue, deadline, plenary, reception. Expression: 'I am looking forward to...' Idiom: 'Time flies.' -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 16 -- Numbers That Matter: Ordinals, Dates, and Time
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Maria Clara | Lesson 16 -- Numbers That Matter: Ordinals, Dates, and Time

listenings.text: Hi Maria Clara, it is Bruno. I am calling to confirm the agenda for Seoul, so please write this down. The opening ceremony is on the seventh, at nine in the morning, on the third floor. The first plenary session starts at ten. The keynote speech is on the eighth. The welcome reception is on the tenth, at seven in the evening, so please do not miss it. And the deadline for the final report is on the fourteenth, not the fifteenth. One more thing: the venue changed. It is now the main university hall, not the small room. Call me if you have any questions. See you in November.

Let me read you my agenda for Seoul. We arrive in November. First, the opening ceremony is on the seventh, in the morning. Then the first plenary session begins at ten. On the eighth, I listen to the keynote speech. After that, there is a working session on the ninth. The welcome reception is on the tenth, in the evening, and I am looking forward to that one. The deadline for our report is on the fourteenth. And on the fifteenth, we have the final session. In September this was only an idea. Now it is on the calendar, with real dates and real times. Time flies. And this time, I will speak for my institution myself.

paras: Maria Clara opens the official agenda for the visit to Seoul. The delegation arrives in November. The program starts on the seventh and finishes on the fifteenth. She reads it slowly, one line at a time, and writes the times in her notebook.

"The opening ceremony is on the seventh, at nine in the morning, on the third floor," she reads. "Then the first plenary session starts at ten. The venue is the main university hall." She underlines the word venue, because last week it was a different room.

"First, there is the keynote speech on the eighth. After that, a working session on the ninth. The welcome reception is on the tenth, at seven in the evening. The deadline for our final report is on the fourteenth." Every event has a day and a time now.

She closes the notebook and smiles. In September the trip was only an idea. Now it is on the calendar, with real dates and real times. Time flies. "I am looking forward to the final session on the fifteenth," she thinks, "and this time, I will speak for my institution myself."
reading.source: Institutional & Travel English -- narrative written for Maria Clara's package

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of this text?
choices: Maria Clara is cancelling the trip to Seoul because the dates changed.
Maria Clara is reading her official Seoul agenda and noting the day and time of each event.
Maria Clara is booking flights and a hotel for a holiday in Seoul.

The opening ceremony is in the afternoon.

The welcome reception is on the tenth, in the evening.

Maria Clara will speak for her institution herself.

bank.label: Reading and saying dates, times, and the order of events
items: The opening ceremony is on the seventh, at nine in the morning.
The conference is in November.
The first plenary session starts at ten o'clock.
First, the keynote. Then, the working session. After that, the reception.
The deadline for the report is on the fourteenth.
I am looking forward to the final session on the fifteenth.

items.situation: A colleague asks when the opening ceremony is. Tell them the day and the time.
tips: The opening ceremony is on the seventh, at nine in the morning.

items.situation: Someone asks in which month the conference in Seoul is. Answer.
Confirm the welcome reception for a colleague: say the date and the time.
tips: The reception is on the tenth, at seven in the evening.

items.situation: A coordinator asks about the deadline for the final report. Answer with the date.
tips: The deadline is on the fourteenth.

items.situation: Say the order of the first three events, using sequence words.
tips: First, the opening ceremony. Then, the plenary session. After that, the keynote speech.

items.situation: Tell a colleague what you are looking forward to, and give the date.
tips: I am looking forward to the welcome reception on the tenth.

pressure.title: Answer live
items: In one sentence: when does the program in Seoul start, and when does it finish? Use dates.
Present your own week now: name three events with the day and the time. Use on and at. Do not stop.
Tell me the Seoul agenda from the seventh to the fifteenth, in order, with sequence words. Thirty seconds.

pressure.text: IN + month or year (in November, in 2027). ON + day or date (on Monday, on the seventh, on November 7th). AT + clock time (at ten o'clock, at 9 a.m.). Dates in American English: Month + ordinal -- November 7th. Sequence: first, then, next, after that, finally.
